prevalence.source: European hemoglobinopathy registry context; WHO Europe migration and hemoglobinopathy burden syntheses
prevalence.trend: stable to increasing in some countries due to migration patterns and improved survival

incidence.value: Varies widely by country; newborn screening present in some regions and high-risk groups
incidence.source: European screening program summaries and hemoglobinopathy registries

burden.dalyPerCapita: low overall; high per-patient burden (pain crises, organ damage, hospitalizations)
burden.mortalityRate: Elevated vs general population; reduced with comprehensive care and disease-modifying therapy

riskFactors: limited access to comprehensive care (for outcomes)
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Newborn screening and confirmatory hemoglobin electrophoresis/HPLC; ongoing monitoring for stroke, pulmonary hypertension and renal disease

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: variable across countries; targeted screening and registry-based follow-up in many systems
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Tens to low hundreds of thousands across the EU
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate to high with comprehensive care, hydroxyurea and specialist follow-up

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Specialist hemoglobinopathy centers in many countries; newborn screening and early referral varies
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Vaccination and infection prophylaxis, hydroxyurea, transfusions and chelation when indicated, pain management pathways, stroke prevention, multidisciplinary pregnancy care
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Cross-country variation in screening and access to comprehensive SCD centers; transition care gaps
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: High availability of specialist care and disease-modifying therapies in many EU health systems
